name: Uddyotakara
dates_notes: Dated by his explicit replies to Diṅnāga (c. 480 CE) and by Dharmakīrti's (c. 600–660 CE) replies in turn; Vidyabhusana 1920 and Matilal 1977 converge on the late sixth or early seventh century. Uddyotakara identifies himself as a Pāśupata Śaiva in the colophon of the Vārttika.

lineage_polemical.note: The Nyāya-Vārttika is largely structured as a reply to Dignāga's Pramāṇa-Samuccaya: Uddyotakara defends the Naiyāyika four-pramāṇa scheme against the pramāṇa-dvaya thesis, the savikalpaka-pratyakṣa analysis against the kalpanā-apoḍha account, and jāti-realism against the apoha-vāda. The Nyāya–Buddhist epistemological exchange has Dignāga as one canonical pole.

Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇa-Vārttika is the Buddhist counter-reply to Uddyotakara's Nyāya-Vārttika: the Naiyāyika defence of the four pramāṇas, of jāti-realism, and of the Naiyāyika analysis of the inferential mark is engaged point-by-point and answered from the developed Buddhist-pramāṇa standpoint.

core_thesis: Uddyotakara's *Nyāya-Vārttika* defends Vātsyāyana's *Bhāṣya* against the Buddhist epistemological challenge mounted by Diṅnāga in the *Pramāṇa-Samuccaya*, and in doing so re-engineers Naiyāyika *pramāṇa*-doctrine into the form that becomes standard. Three contributions matter. First, the analysis of *anumāna*: Uddyotakara reformulates the *triple-mark* (*tri-rūpa-liṅga*) requirement on a Naiyāyika realist basis (against Diṅnāga, *Pramāṇa-Samuccaya* 2.1–8 on the *trairūpya*) and develops the *hetv-ābhāsa* (fallacious reasons) classification — *asiddha*, *viruddha*, *anaikāntika*, *prakaraṇa-sama*, *kālātīta* — that the school carries forward (*Nyāya-Vārttika* on Nyāya-Sūtra 1.2.4–9). The treatment of *sapakṣa* and *vipakṣa* — the relevant similar and dissimilar instances — is given here in the form Vāchaspati Miśra and Udayana inherit. Second, the defence of *jāti*: Uddyotakara's reply to Diṅnāga's *apoha-vāda* (the Buddhist nominalist thesis that 'cow' refers by exclusion of non-cows; *Pramāṇa-Samuccaya* 5.1–11) is the locus classicus for the Naiyāyika realist position that universals are mind-independent samavāya-related entities cognised in perception itself. The *apoha-jāti* debate is centred at *Nyāya-Vārttika* on NS 2.2.66. Third, the analysis of *śabda-pramāṇa*: Uddyotakara's defence of testimony as an independent *pramāṇa* against Buddhist reduction of testimony to inference (against *Pramāṇa-Samuccaya* chapter 5) becomes the standard Naiyāyika position (*Nyāya-Vārttika* on NS 2.1.49–68). The *Vārttika*'s historical importance is generative: Vāchaspati Miśra's *Tātparya-Ṭīkā* takes it as base, Udayana's *Tātparya-Pariśuddhi* sub-commentates Vāchaspati on the *Vārttika*, and the entire mature Old-Nyāya stack is the *Vārttika*-stream as preserved through these sub-commentaries.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Varttika
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composition dated by explicit Diṅnāga-replies and by Dharmakīrti's later engagement.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary on Vātsyāyana's Bhāṣya; its colophon identifies Uddyotakara as a Pāśupata.
engaged_works.summary: *Vārttika*-style sub-commentary on Vātsyāyana's *Nyāya-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*, working through the *Sūtra* and *Bhāṣya* together. The polemical occasion is Diṅnāga's *Pramāṇa-Samuccaya* (esp. chapter 1 on *pratyakṣa* and chapter 5 on *apoha*): Uddyotakara opens with a *maṅgala* whose explicit purpose is to defend the *Bhāṣya* against the criticisms it has accumulated. The bulk of the polemical work happens at three loci. *Nyāya-Vārttika* on NS 1.1.4 on *pratyakṣa* — Uddyotakara reformulates the perceptual definition to handle Diṅnāga's objections about *kalpanāpoḍha* (free-of-conceptual-construction) perception (against *Pramāṇa-Samuccaya* 1.3–4). *Nyāya-Vārttika* on NS 1.1.5 (with NS 1.2.4–9) on *anumāna* — the *tri-rūpa-liṅga* analysis is reset on a realist footing and the *hetv-ābhāsa* fivefold typology (*asiddha*, *viruddha*, *anaikāntika*, *prakaraṇa-sama*, *kālātīta*) is developed. *Nyāya-Vārttika* on NS 2.2.66 on *jāti* — the *apoha*-vs-realist-universal debate (against *Pramāṇa-Samuccaya* 5.1–11) is centred here in its classical form. The *Vārttika*'s technical achievement is to absorb Buddhist epistemological pressure without conceding the realist ontology; its historical achievement is to fix the form in which the *Sūtra*-*Bhāṣya* are read in the medieval Nyāya school.
